chapters.title: Mother Earth isn't just a literal expression
chapters.text:
Scientists have been studying nature for centuries, yet it continues to bring surprises. For instance, recent research has discovered that the scent of humus makes us release oxytocin. Usually, this hormone strengthens the bond between the closest people, like mother and child. Why do our brains react to humus this way? What if our current beliefs about the environment are inexact?

In Western culture, we see the world through the lens of hierarchy. People are incomparable, occupying the top position while others are beneath them. Unsurprisingly, we often treat animals, plants, or land as resources. We all know the consequences of this attitude: global warming, deforestation, over-fishing, extinction of species, tons of waste, and the like. It's high time for us to rethink our relationship with nature.

What is the alternative approach? Native Americans believe people are the youngest on Earth; therefore, they should learn from other beings. Let's look at plants. They provide for themselves and sustain life on the whole planet. When insects attack a tree, it manufactures pheromones, warning others and encouraging them to produce defensive chemicals. Forests also have “social media” — fungal strands in soil. These networks resemble Robin Hood. They distribute the carbohydrates equally among all the trees. Impressive, right?

Initially, we were all aware of nature's wisdom and had a respectful attitude toward it. Yet we have abandoned this knowledge while adjusting to capitalistic society's rules. It's mostly forgotten but not lost. Native Americans remember it. So do our brains as reactions to humus confirm. Join our eye-opening expedition to recall the principles that will help to save the Earth for future generations.

The most powerful tool to protect the environment is changing how we perceive nature.

chapters.title: Science is a crucial source of knowledge, but not the only one
chapters.text:
It took Robin Wall Kimmerer a long time to remember nature's wisdom. Although she is a member of the Potawatomi (a Native American nation), she had to adjust to Western standards during her studies. Many professors encouraged her to concentrate only on scientific data, stating that stories from Kimmerer's elders weren't based on evidence.

Native people strive to understand nature with four aspects: mind, body, emotion, and spirit. In Western science, only the first two components are essential.

But then she received an opportunity to test Potawatomi's knowledge. Local communities turned to botanists to investigate why the number of sweetgrasses, the nation’s sacred plant, declined. Many researchers supposed the reason was harvesting. Therefore, they recommended giving the plant a break. Kimmerer wasn't sure: the Potawatomi believe sweetgrass leaves when people neglect it. She decided to figure out this case with her graduate student, Laurie. After a few years of collecting data, they revealed the actual reason. It was the opposite of the widely held scientific view: the harvested sweetgrass thrived, while the one undisturbed degraded. So, picking plants facilitated its faster growth — indigenous knowledge was exact.

People often believe that only science has all the answers and disregard everything that doesn't belong to it as unimportant. As a result, they may become hostages of tunnel vision. For example, farmers decide to grow corn in their fields. Seeing insects and weeds, they spray crops with pesticides. In case soil lacks nitrogen, they use fertilizers. It seems the only reasonable way of dealing with these challenges. However, farmers neglect the actual cause: territories with only one species of plants are more susceptible to pest outbreaks. What is the solution, then?

Native Americans have a legend about Three Sisters — corn, beans, and squash. This story is an ancient guide, not just “folklore,” stating that these three plants are an ideal combination. The squash covers the land, decreasing weeds. The corn grows the fastest and becomes a support for beans. The latter enriches the soil with mineral nitrogen that squash and corn need. Together they don't require any pesticides to thrive. Instead of competing, they complement each other. This is the balance modern farmers should strive to achieve.

For Kimmerer, it's also a metaphor. Similarly, scientific and indigenous knowledge can't exist as separate entities. We will strengthen our communities only by combining them. Let's look at Native American beliefs that are incredibly precious today.

chapters.title: Democracy of species is at the core of the indigenous outlook
chapters.text:
The key to understanding culture is its language. It reflects the nation's traditions, beliefs, and perspective on the world. For instance, Potawatomi has a word that means “power, facilitating mushrooms to pop up through the soil overnight” — puhpowee. Why did this detail draw the attention of the nation's ancestors and receive a particular name? Kimmerer was eager to find out and learn the tongue of her people.

In some Native languages the term for plants translates to “those who take care of us.” ~ Robin Wall Kimmerer, Dr.

She lives away from the reservation but regularly visits during the yearly tribal gathering. During one of them, she attended a language class. Kimmerer was astounded to discover that there were only nine fluent speakers of Potawatomi. Most of them were older people. Afterward, she immediately signed up for online language revival classes.

In the beginning, it was child's play for her. As she studied the Latin names of plants, she got used to memorizing thousands of nouns. Yet, unlike English, this part of speech isn't dominant in the Potawatomi language. Verbs constitute 70% of its vocabulary, and each of those verbs conjugates. Besides, their meanings are unusual — for instance, “to be a river.” Kimmerer, bewildered, “Isn't river a noun?” Indeed, English speakers treat it like an object, using the pronoun it. But not the Potawatomi. They classify everything into animate and inanimate. For them, plants, animals, fire, water, rocks, mountains, legends, songs, and many others are alive. The lifeless group is relatively small, comprising material objects made by humans.

What does it mean in practice? The Potawatomi nation speaks about the environment as we talk about people. It's unthinkable to refer to somebody using the pronoun it. This way, we would deprive people of fundamental rights, treating them as objects. Similarly, Native people can't use it for nature. If they ask about a tree, they say, “Who is this being?” They believe the human nation is one of many others inhabiting the planet.

From the Potawatomi perspective, we live in a democracy of species. Humans coexist with Bird people, Plant peoples, and others.

According to the indigenous view, humanity isn't the center of the universe. Each day we have a chance to learn from nations much older than ours. For instance, Kimmerer recalls hearing how elders advised others to observe Beaver people.

chapters.title: Let's replace consumerism with a culture of reciprocity
chapters.text:
Growing up next to nature, Kimmerer has always remembered that it provides us with gifts, not resources. In the Western world, a standard definition of a present is “something given for free.” Yet for Native Americans, it goes hand in hand with responsibility — to sustain the bond started by the gift. For example, Kimmerer once conducted research in the Andes. After work, she planned to visit a local market and prepared a long list of products. Yet the vendors didn't take money from her, considering papayas or tomatoes as gifts. Therefore, Kimmerer restrained herself in shopping to not take advantage of their kindness. Returning to the hotel, she pondered the presents she would prepare for vendors in return.

Potawatomi's outlook from old times has entrenched the principle of responsibility. First settlers were astounded by the Native people’s enormous wild rice harvest. They collected such a significant amount in a few days that it would be enough for a year. At the same time, colonists wondered why so much rice stood unreaped. They couldn't understand that it wasn't laziness. Indigenous people left it intentionally to let plants seed themselves and feed animals who lived nearby. The Potawatomi see their part of the harvest as half at most.

The core indigenous belief states what is beneficial for land is good for people.

The guidelines Native people follow aren't from books. They are called principles of the Honorable Harvest, and Potawatomi spread them by word of mouth. Some of them, highly relevant today, include the following:

• Respond to Earth's presents by taking care of nature.
• Take the amount that you genuinely need.
• Don't touch the first or the last. The Potawatomi gather a plant only when they are sure it's not the only one in the forest.
• Take yields with minimum harm.
• Treat nature's gifts with respect and never waste them.
• Share presents with those who need them the most.

All these guidelines support a culture of reciprocity. It encourages us to think about what we can do for nature in return for its generosity.

Nowadays, our consumerist habits are far from these principles. We get used to paying for products and not bothering about the field they came from. Yet everything is a question of our perception. We choose to treat the world as a gift or take it for granted.

chapters.title: How gratitude can protect the environment
chapters.text:
If we visit a school of Native people at the beginning or end of the week, we will witness another core element of indigenous culture. It logically stems from seeing the world as a gift and is called the Thanksgiving Address.

Firstly, children express gratitude to the earth, which provides us with everything. Despite being wounded by humans, it continues to support us. Secondly, they thank water. Then, fish, food plants, medicine herbs, trees, and the like. Westerners sometimes witness a Thanksgiving Address and suppose the list is too long. Kimmerer only sympathizes with them. They can’t even imagine how much we can be grateful for.

This habit has revolutionary power. It shows people how wealthy they are, having more than enough. Isn't it an antidote to consumer society? The latter rests on scarcity, telling people what they don't have, like a new video game or smartphone model. It aims to create unmet desires because that's how it earns money. Moreover, it persuades us that only commodities bring satisfaction. As a result, we believe our happiness depends on the number of possessions. On the contrary, the Thanksgiving Address contributes to the ethic of fullness. It's a culture of gratitude's credo and effective medicine for our attitude toward nature.

What's more, instead of highlighting our autonomy, it concentrates on our interdependence. Philosophers have warned us about a special type of sadness — “species loneliness.” It's the consequence of separation from nature and placing ourselves at the top of the hierarchy. Yet it was our choice. So, we can change it — thank Mother Earth and pick up where we have left off. This small habit may become a step toward transforming our societies into cultures of gratitude.

Cutting ourselves off the land, people forget how to live as a part of the whole. This way, they condemn themselves to loneliness.

Additionally, discussing how the sun, rain, plants, and others care about us provides a priceless feeling — nature loves us. We can experience this feeling in another way — by growing something. That's why when the media ask Kimmerer how to return the balance between humans and the environment, she recommends planting a garden and caring about it. By cultivating a connection to it and feeling its love in response, we will establish a passion for the whole of nature.

chapters.title: Restoration of Earth and our attitude to it is a promising solution
chapters.text:
Kimmerer once asked her students to name humanity’s adverse effects on nature. There were dozens of answers. Yet when she offered them to recall positive ones, everyone was silent. She was astounded. How does society want to build sustainability if it can't imagine what it looks like?

In a sense, we should blame our narrative about the environmental crisis for that. Showing ecosystem loss appeals to fear, pity, and despair. The latter is the worst as it dashes our hope to change the situation. But our tears won't save the planet; only action can.

Kimmerer believes restoration can solve our despair and be a to-do list for saving wounded land. Yet it should be the correct one. For instance, laws obliging mining and other industries that have ruined land to plant vegetation aren't effective. Restoration means returning the territory to its state before exploitation. If a forest was in this place, companies should plant all the species that grew in it. The key is functional integrity, not merely recreating a form.

Another pitfall to avoid is a partial solution. What does a typical fix look like? People might plant willows nearby to protect toxic chemicals from leaking into rivers. Indeed, this species is the best one to absorb water. Yet monoculture rarely works in nature, as we have discussed with corn. This mistake roots in the belief that we know better how to restore ecosystems.

Native people suppose that plants are the best land healers. We should learn from them.

Looking at waste beds or other wounded territories, we notice who provides the earth with first aid. Dandelions, legumes, ragweed, clovers, and other species make the soil more appropriate for other plants. Soon, some trees accompany them, as well as ants, bugs, and birds. Gradually they heal the land, giving us an example of how to do it. We can join these forces.

We need acts of restoration, not only for polluted waters and degraded lands, but also for our relationship to the world. ~ Robin Wall Kimmerer, Dr.

At the same time, the most crucial task is to restore respect, care, and responsibility in our communication with nature. Without it, hardly any positive changes are possible.

chapters.title: We could lose Potawatomi's perspective on nature
chapters.text:
Kimmerer's people had to leave their land twice during the life of one generation. Initially, they lived near Lake Michigan, but settlers cast eyes on these territories. As a result, Potawatomi moved from Wisconsin to Kansas under the control of soldiers with guns. Yet it wasn't enough for colonists. Soon they offered Native people citizenship and private property in Oklahoma, saying that this time no one could take it away from them.

Although it meant breaking with the usual way of life, leaders agreed; they had no choice. Without citizenship, they could lose their lands in Kansas at any time. However, living in Oklahoma didn't guarantee the safety of their lands. There were many pretexts to deprive Native people of what belonged to them, for instance, in case they didn't pay taxes.

Potawatomi lost over 66% of the received territories during 1887–1934.

These challenges didn't undermine the nation's identity. Therefore, officials used other methods. Firstly, settlers changed all the names they found difficult to pronounce. Kimmerer's great-grandmother Sha-note became Charlotte. The same holds for toponyms. For instance, “the Cloudsplitter” in the Adirondacks was called Mount Marcy. Settlers dedicated it to a governor who had never visited that place.

At the same time, the separation of children from their families was the most effective tool in terms of assimilation. All over territories where Native people lived, agents gathered children to send them to boarding schools. Officials even received signed documents from parents who “agreed” to let their kids go study. Yet it was fake consent. If they refused to do it, they could go to prison. At the same time, some parents approved education, hoping to protect children from hunger or guarantee a better future. Kimmerer didn't know the decisive factor for her family, but her grandmother also signed these papers.

In boarding schools, children couldn't speak their language and had to forget about their traditions. That's a fundamental reason why only nine people are now fluent speakers of the Potawatomi. One recalled how he hid so agents didn't catch him.

Nevertheless, even these measures didn't break the spirit of Potawatomi. The nation has saved its identity and now works to restore American lands.

Did you know? Estimates of the early 21st century indicate approximately 26,000 people of Potawatomi descent.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Sweetgrass takes a peculiar place in Potawatomi's heart: it's one of four sacred plants. For this nation, braiding its long and shiny strands resembles the braiding of Mother Earth's hair. Besides, it's always a moment of reciprocity: a person needs someone to help with this mission. The best stories and the warmest memories are born at this time. What's more, the Potawatomi believe if we breathe sweetgrass's fragrance, we will remember knowledge we don't know we forgot. The braid of Kimmerer's stories tries to do the same — remind us about the attitude toward Earth that we have neglected.

Many of us need this help. Urban lifestyle, consumerist society, and commercialization cut people off their natural habitat. Now we live in concrete jungles, often feeling lonely. We have forgotten the bond with nature. Moreover, we started to take its gifts for granted, devaluing signs of its love. Placing ourselves on the top of the hierarchy, we stopped learning from teachers who have existed on Earth for far longer.

Fortunately, this knowledge is preserved. Deep inside ourselves, we remember who we are, and there are those, like the Potawatomi Nation, who can share this wisdom with us. Now it's time to recall what it means to be genuinely a part of nature. It has been our tower of strength all this time, but now it requires our help.

The first step is to change our relationship with Mother Earth.

Try this


• Bring a part of nature into your home — take care of an indoor plant or herbs for the kitchen. If possible, start a garden.
• Visit the local market and talk to farmers. Their love of the land will inspire you.
• Investigate what plants are most common in your neighborhood.
